title: match-case statement

overview.what:
match-case (Python 3.10+) is structural pattern matching — it compares a subject against the STRUCTURE of each case, not just its equality. A capture pattern binds a name from inside the matched structure:

  def route(event):
      match event:
          case {'type': 'login', 'user': user}:
              return f'login by {user}'
          case {'type': 'logout'}:
              return 'logout'
          case _:
              return 'unknown'

The first case matches any dict that has both a 'type' key equal to 'login' AND a 'user' key; the value under 'user' is captured into the variable user. The wildcard _ is the catch-all — it matches anything and binds nothing. Like if/elif, only the FIRST matching case runs. You can also OR literal values with |: case 'GET' | 'HEAD': matches either string.
overview.why: match-case replaces cascading isinstance checks and multi-key dict lookups when dispatching on message types or API events. Without it, routing a dozen event types requires a long if/elif chain full of repeated dict.get() calls.

why_learning_this: Replacing cascading isinstance/dict-key if/elif chains with declarative structural dispatch on API payloads
Reading modern Python 3.10+ code that uses pattern matching on JSON events and command structs

common_mistakes.title: Expecting a bare variable name to compare, not capture
common_mistakes.explanation: case status_code: does NOT compare against an existing variable — it captures the subject into a new name status_code. To compare against a variable, use a guard: case x if x == status_code:.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting the wildcard and getting no match
common_mistakes.explanation: If no case matches and there is no _ catch-all, match silently does nothing — there is no error. Always add a case _: branch for unexpected values.

common_mistakes.title: Using match for simple equality dispatch
common_mistakes.explanation: match is most valuable for structural patterns (dicts, nested objects). For plain value equality on a string or int, a dict lookup or if/elif is simpler and more readable.

recall_questions.q: What does the wildcard `_` do in a match-case statement?
recall_questions.answer: It matches anything and is the catch-all branch; unlike other patterns it does not bind the matched value to a variable.

recall_questions.q: What happens when a capture pattern like `user` appears inside a dict pattern?
recall_questions.answer: The value at that key in the matched dict is bound to the variable user, making it available inside that case block.

recall_questions.q: What happens if no case branch matches and there is no wildcard?
recall_questions.answer: match silently does nothing — there is no error or exception.

recall_questions.q: How do you match either of two literal values in a single case?
recall_questions.answer: Use the | operator between literals, e.g. case 'GET' | 'HEAD':.

practice_tasks.title: HTTP method dispatcher
practice_tasks.prompt: Write dispatch(method) that uses match-case to return 'read' for 'GET', 'write' for 'POST' or 'PUT', 'delete' for 'DELETE', and 'unsupported' for anything else. Print results for GET, POST, and PATCH.
practice_tasks.solution:
def dispatch(method):
    match method:
        case 'GET':
            return 'read'
        case 'POST' | 'PUT':
            return 'write'
        case 'DELETE':
            return 'delete'
        case _:
            return 'unsupported'

print(dispatch('GET'))
print(dispatch('POST'))
print(dispatch('PATCH'))

code_walkthrough.code:
def handle(event):
    match event:
        case {'type': 'signup', 'email': email}:
            return f'new user: {email}'
        case {'type': 'logout'}:
            return 'logged out'
        case _:
            return 'unknown'

print(handle({'type': 'signup', 'email': 'a@b.com'}))
print(handle({'type': 'logout'}))
print(handle({'type': 'ping'}))
code_walkthrough.focus: The first case matches the dict's structure AND binds the 'email' value to a variable; the second matches on type alone; _ catches anything unmatched.

challenge.title: API event router
challenge.prompt: Write route_event(event) that handles dict payloads. If type='db_query' and a 'table' key is present, return 'querying <table>'. If type='cache_clear', return 'clearing cache'. If type='healthcheck', return 'ok'. For any other type, return 'unhandled: <type>'. Test all four patterns.
challenge.solution:
def route_event(event):
    match event:
        case {'type': 'db_query', 'table': table}:
            return f'querying {table}'
        case {'type': 'cache_clear'}:
            return 'clearing cache'
        case {'type': 'healthcheck'}:
            return 'ok'
        case {'type': t}:
            return f'unhandled: {t}'

print(route_event({'type': 'db_query', 'table': 'users'}))
print(route_event({'type': 'cache_clear'}))
print(route_event({'type': 'healthcheck'}))
print(route_event({'type': 'unknown'}))
